title: CCI Snapshot - Discovery Test
subtitle: Snapshot tab + Opportunity Cards
description: Test whether the Snapshot tab helps users find where to focus and navigate to opportunity cards. Learn what they use, what they ignore, and what is missing - particularly on the left panel and the sorting tabs.

rules: We are testing the product, not you. There are no wrong answers.
Please think out loud - tell me what you are looking at and what you are thinking.
If you get stuck, tell me what you expected to happen.
I may stay quiet so I do not bias you.
setup.noHelpPolicy: Give 10 seconds of silence before prompting. If stuck, ask: What would you do next if I was not here? Do NOT point to UI elements or explain what things do. If they ask is this right - say: What do you think?

warmup.intro: Ask these before showing the prototype. Goal: understand their current mental model before the product shapes it.

questions.label: Current process
questions.question: When you are identifying cost saving opportunities - where do you typically start? What are the top 2-3 things you look for?
questions.listenFor: What data matters to them? What makes something worth pursuing?

questions.label: Recent example
questions.question: Think of the last cost initiative you moved forward. What made you confident it was worth pursuing?
questions.listenFor: What evidence do they need? Who else was involved?

tasks.name: First impressions
tasks.subtitle: Observe before they click anything
tasks.script: Take a moment to look at this page without clicking anything. Tell me what you are looking at. What is this page showing you, and where would you focus first?
watchFor: What catches their eye first - left panel, right panel, summary cards at top, or the AI banner?
Do they read the left panel as a filter, a summary, or something else entirely?
Do they notice New / Active / Dismissed without being told what they mean?
Do they understand the category breakdown (Supplies, Routine, Ancillary)?
Do they understand the page purpose without any explanation?
tasks.success: User can describe what the page is for in their own words. Points to a specific area and gives a reason tied to what they see.
tasks.learnWhy: Pure orientation. Tests basic comprehension before any interaction biases them. Also tells you whether the left/right split reads as two connected things or two unrelated panels.

tasks.name: Natural first click
tasks.subtitle: Where do they go first?
tasks.script: Now, click the first thing you’d explore on this page. As you do, tell me what you expect will happen
watchFor: Where do they click first: left panel, right panel, or AI banner?
Do they start by selecting a category on the left or a card on the right?
Do they understand left-side selections filter the right side?
Does the click outcome match their expectation? If not, what did they expect?
Do they understand the panels' filtering logic?
tasks.success: User clicks first with a clear reason, states what they expect to happen. The outcome makes sense to them (no major confusion).
tasks.learnWhy: Shows natural entry path and whether they use left-side filtering or skip it.

tasks.name: Find the biggest opportunity
tasks.subtitle: Test left panel and sorting tabs together
tasks.script: How would you use this page to find the biggest cost-savings opportunity right now? Walk me through your steps.
watchFor: First path: left panel filter or right-side list?
Do they notice/use the Show tabs?
Do they realize tabs change ranking (same items)?
Do they rely on the AI banner vs exploring?
End state: confident choice or hesitation?
tasks.success: Picks one top opportunity, explains why, and uses a tab or filter to confirm (not just the default top row).
tasks.learnWhy: Tests Snapshot’s core value: can users triage fast and understand Show tabs as re-ranking the same items.
tasks.followUpProbe: If they stop at Annual Savings without exploring other tabs, ask: Did you consider looking at this any other way?

tasks.name: Explore the Recurring tab
tasks.subtitle: Discoverability and comprehension
tasks.script: “Do you ever look for patterns that repeat — like the same procedures or providers showing up across multiple cost saving opportunities?” “If yes, where would you look on this page to find that?”
watchFor: Do they find the Recurring tab without being directed to it?
Once on Recurring - do they understand what the three sections show (Procedures / Providers / Supplies)?
Do they understand what the numbers mean - for example 3 high-cost providers?
Is Recurring a meaningful label to them, or does it need more explanation?
Which section do they look at first - Procedures, Providers, or Supplies?
Do they find the hover tooltips, and do they help?
tasks.success: User finds the Recurring tab. Can explain in their own words what at least one of the three sections is showing. Can identify at least one item they would act on and why.
tasks.learnWhy: Tests discoverability of the tab and comprehension of the concept. Which section they reach for first tells us which lens matters most to this user type.

tasks.name: View All and navigate to a card
tasks.subtitle: Modal and jump to Opportunity Hub
tasks.script: You want to see everything - not just the top 3. Find the full list and pick one opportunity you would want to look into.
watchFor: Do they find the View All link without prompting?
Once in the modal - do they feel oriented, or overwhelmed?
Do they use the bar chart or switch to the table? Which do they prefer?
Can they find the icon that jumps to the opportunity card?
Does the modal feel like a natural extension of the page, or a separate tool?
tasks.success: User opens View All, browses the list, and successfully navigates to an opportunity card. Does not get lost or confused about where they are.
tasks.learnWhy: Tests the View All modal and the path from Snapshot to Opportunity Hub. If users feel the modal is a full analytics tool rather than a simple list, that is a signal it is still too complex.

tasks.name: Read and interpret a Supply card
tasks.subtitle: Comprehension and trust
tasks.script: Take a look at this opportunity. Tell me what it is showing you. If you needed to decide whether this is worth pursuing - what would you look at, and what would you need to feel confident?
watchFor: What do they read first - savings amount, provider comparison, or cost drivers?
Do they understand the high-cost vs low-cost provider comparison?
Do they understand use-driven vs price-driven without explanation?
Do they trust the savings estimate, or question how it was calculated?
Do they expand the card to see more detail, or stay in collapsed view?
What would make them confident enough to bring this to a physician conversation?
tasks.success: User can describe: what the cost gap is, who the high-cost provider is, and at least one driver. Can state whether they would act on it and why.
tasks.learnWhy: Tests card comprehension and trust for Supply opportunities. The trust question is critical - if they understand but do not trust it, that is a different problem than not understanding.

tasks.name: Read a Routine Services card
tasks.subtitle: First draft - exploratory only
tasks.script: Now look at this one. It is a different type of opportunity. Tell me what it is showing you and what the numbers mean to you.
watchFor: Do they connect length of stay to the dollar figure without explanation?
Do they understand what the CMS benchmark means and why it matters?
Does the LOS gap concept make sense, or does it need a plain-language explanation?
Who do they think should own this type of opportunity?
What is confusing, missing, or surprising?
Would they act on this card, or do they need more information first?
tasks.success: User can describe the LOS gap and the dollar implication in their own words. Notes what makes sense and what does not. States who they would bring this to.
tasks.learnWhy: Exploratory only - this is a first draft. We want impressions and gaps, not pass/fail. The most valuable output is what they say is missing or confusing.

facilitatorNote.title: Routine Services card - first draft
facilitatorNote.content: Do not guide or explain. After they have explored, probe with: What would you need to see to trust this number? / Who would you bring this to? / What is missing here for you?

wrapup.intro: Ask after all tasks. These are discussion questions - not tasks. Let them talk.

questions.label: Overall
questions.question: Now that you have seen the whole flow - what worked? What was frustrating or unclear?

questions.label: Missing information
questions.question: Was there anything you expected to see that was not there?

questions.label: Trust
questions.question: Would you use this to prepare for a conversation with a physician or a finance review? Why or why not?

questions.label: Left panel
questions.question: On the main screen - did you find the left side useful? Would you use it, or would you go straight to the cards?

observerNotes.meaning: Left panel may not communicate its filtering role - test whether a label or affordance change helps
Tabs are not prominent enough, or Annual Savings default is sufficient and others are not needed

observerNotes.behavior: Thinks tabs show different content rather than different sorting
observerNotes.meaning: Tab labels need clearer differentiation - consider adding a one-line description under each

observerNotes.behavior: Does not find Recurring tab or is confused by it
observerNotes.meaning: Tab label or content needs work - probe: What would you call this?

observerNotes.behavior: Feels View All modal is overwhelming
observerNotes.meaning: Modal is still too complex - consider stripping to list only with no internal tabs

observerNotes.behavior: Cannot get from Snapshot to a card
observerNotes.meaning: Navigation path is broken - the jump icon or View All link is not clear enough

observerNotes.behavior: Understands Supply card but does not trust it
observerNotes.meaning: Trust problem, not comprehension problem - probe what would increase confidence

observerNotes.behavior: Cannot connect LOS to dollars on Routine card
observerNotes.meaning: The translation needs a plain-language bridge - X extra days = Y dollars per case may need to be explicit

observerNotes.behavior: Says I would need to talk to someone for Routine card
observerNotes.meaning: Card does not give enough to act - probe exactly what is missing
